headline: Union Budget 2026: GDP growth projected at 7.8%, fiscal deficit target at 4.5%
body: Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman presented the Union Budget 2026 today, projecting India's GDP growth at 7.8% for FY27, up from 7.2% in the current fiscal. The government has set an ambitious fiscal deficit target of 4.5% of GDP, down from 5.1% in FY26. The budget focuses on capital expenditure with an allocation of Rs 11.1 lakh crore, representing a 15% increase from the previous year. Key infrastructure projects including highways, railways, and digital infrastructure will receive priority funding. The Finance Minister emphasized that this budget aims to make India a developed nation by 2047, with a focus on sustainable and inclusive growth. Tax revenues are expected to grow by 12% year-on-year, driven by improved compliance and economic expansion. The government also announced measures to boost manufacturing through Production Linked Incentive schemes across 14 sectors.

headline: Budget 2026: Capital expenditure gets Rs 11.1 lakh crore boost, infrastructure focus intensifies
body: The Union Budget 2026 has allocated a record Rs 11.1 lakh crore for capital expenditure, marking a 15% increase from the previous fiscal year. This massive infrastructure push is expected to create millions of jobs and accelerate economic growth. The allocation includes Rs 2.7 lakh crore for railways, Rs 2.1 lakh crore for highways and roads, and Rs 1.5 lakh crore for urban infrastructure development. The government plans to develop 50 new airports and expand metro rail networks in 25 cities. Additionally, Rs 75,000 crore has been earmarked for green energy projects, including solar parks and electric vehicle charging infrastructure. Economists believe this capex-heavy budget will have a multiplier effect on the economy, boosting demand for cement, steel, and other construction materials. The Finance Minister stated that infrastructure development is critical for sustaining high growth rates and improving logistics efficiency.

headline: Tax reforms in Budget 2026: New income tax slabs announced, standard deduction raised to Rs 75,000
body: The Union Budget 2026 has introduced significant tax reforms aimed at putting more money in the hands of middle-class taxpayers. The standard deduction has been increased from Rs 50,000 to Rs 75,000, benefiting salaried individuals and pensioners. A new income tax regime with revised slabs has been proposed: income up to Rs 3 lakh remains tax-free, Rs 3-7 lakh taxed at 5%, Rs 7-10 lakh at 10%, Rs 10-12 lakh at 15%, Rs 12-15 lakh at 20%, and above Rs 15 lakh at 30%. The old tax regime with deductions will continue to be available as an option. Corporate tax rates remain unchanged at 22% for domestic companies. The budget also proposes to simplify GST compliance with quarterly returns for small businesses. Angel tax has been abolished to boost startup funding. Tax experts estimate that these changes will leave an additional Rs 40,000 in the hands of middle-income taxpayers annually.

headline: Budget 2026 targets Rs 50 lakh crore disinvestment, Air India stake sale on cards
body: The government has set an ambitious disinvestment target of Rs 50 lakh crore for FY27, aiming to reduce its stake in public sector enterprises. The budget document reveals plans to divest stakes in Air India, BPCL, Shipping Corporation, and several other PSUs. The Finance Minister emphasized that strategic disinvestment will improve efficiency and unlock value for taxpayers. Proceeds from disinvestment will be used to fund infrastructure projects and reduce government debt. The government also plans to list several subsidiaries of profitable PSUs on stock exchanges. LIC is expected to see further stake dilution through follow-on public offerings. Experts believe that successful execution of this disinvestment program could significantly improve India's fiscal position. However, opposition parties have criticized the move, arguing that selling profitable PSUs is short-sighted. The government maintains that private sector participation will bring better management and global competitiveness.

headline: Budget 2026: Agriculture sector gets Rs 1.5 lakh crore boost, MSP increase announced
body: The agriculture sector has received significant attention in Budget 2026 with an allocation of Rs 1.5 lakh crore, up 20% from the previous year. The government announced a 10% increase in Minimum Support Prices (MSP) for 23 crops, benefiting millions of farmers. A new scheme worth Rs 25,000 crore will provide interest-free loans to farmer producer organizations (FPOs). The budget also allocates Rs 15,000 crore for developing climate-resilient crop varieties and promoting natural farming. Irrigation infrastructure will receive Rs 40,000 crore to expand coverage from 52% to 65% of cultivable land. The government plans to establish 500 new cold storage facilities and 100 food processing clusters to reduce post-harvest losses. Agricultural exports are expected to reach $100 billion by 2028 with new market access initiatives. Rural employment schemes will receive enhanced funding to provide 150 days of guaranteed work per household.

headline: Healthcare allocation jumps to Rs 1.2 lakh crore, 10,000 new medical seats announced
body: Budget 2026 has prioritized healthcare with an allocation of Rs 1.2 lakh crore, representing a 25% increase from FY26. The government announced plans to establish 10,000 new medical college seats over the next three years to address the doctor shortage. Rs 30,000 crore has been earmarked for upgrading district hospitals with modern equipment and ICU facilities. The Ayushman Bharat scheme will be expanded to cover all citizens above 70 years of age, regardless of income. A new National Mental Health Mission with a budget of Rs 5,000 crore will establish counseling centers in every district. The budget allocates Rs 15,000 crore for vaccine development and pandemic preparedness. Telemedicine infrastructure will receive Rs 8,000 crore to connect rural areas with specialist doctors. The government also announced tax incentives for private hospitals setting up facilities in tier-2 and tier-3 cities. Health insurance premiums will be eligible for higher deductions under income tax.

headline: Budget 2026: Defense budget crosses Rs 7 lakh crore, focus on indigenous manufacturing
body: India's defense budget has crossed Rs 7 lakh crore for the first time, with a strong emphasis on indigenous manufacturing and modernization. The capital acquisition budget stands at Rs 2.8 lakh crore, with 75% reserved for domestic procurement. The government announced plans to establish three new defense industrial corridors in Uttar Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, and Maharashtra. The budget allocates Rs 50,000 crore for developing next-generation fighter aircraft, submarines, and missile systems. The 'Make in India' initiative in defense is expected to create 500,000 jobs over the next five years. Private sector participation in defense manufacturing will be encouraged through tax incentives and streamlined licensing. The budget also provides Rs 25,000 crore for border infrastructure development, including roads, bridges, and surveillance systems in sensitive areas. Veterans' welfare schemes will receive enhanced funding with improved pension benefits and healthcare facilities.

headline: Education budget rises to Rs 1.8 lakh crore, new IITs and IIMs planned
body: The education sector has received a substantial boost with an allocation of Rs 1.8 lakh crore in Budget 2026. The government announced plans to establish five new IITs in Andhra Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. Three new IIMs will be set up in the northeastern states. The budget allocates Rs 40,000 crore for the National Education Policy implementation, focusing on foundational literacy and numeracy. Higher education will see the creation of 200,000 new seats in central universities. Rs 25,000 crore has been earmarked for digital education infrastructure, including providing tablets to 10 million students from economically weaker sections. The PM Scholarship scheme will be expanded to cover 500,000 students annually. Skill development programs will receive Rs 35,000 crore to train 10 million youth in emerging technologies like AI, robotics, and renewable energy. Research and innovation grants for universities have been doubled to Rs 20,000 crore.

headline: Sensex surges 800 points post-budget, banking and infrastructure stocks lead rally
body: Indian stock markets rallied sharply following the Union Budget announcement, with the Sensex gaining 800 points to close at 78,450 and Nifty up 240 points at 23,680. Banking stocks led the surge with HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, and SBI gaining 3-4% on expectations of higher credit growth driven by infrastructure spending. Infrastructure and capital goods stocks were the biggest gainers, with Larsen & Toubro up 6%, Adani Ports up 5%, and Tata Steel up 4.5%. The increased capex allocation and focus on infrastructure development boosted investor sentiment. IT stocks showed mixed performance as there were no major announcements for the sector. FMCG stocks declined slightly due to concerns about rural demand. Foreign institutional investors turned net buyers, pumping in Rs 3,500 crore. Market analysts believe the budget's growth-oriented approach will support continued market momentum. However, some experts cautioned that high valuations remain a concern.

headline: Rupee strengthens to 82.50 against dollar as budget boosts investor confidence
body: The Indian rupee strengthened significantly against the US dollar, appreciating to 82.50 from 83.10 in the previous session, as the Union Budget boosted investor confidence. The fiscal consolidation roadmap and strong growth projections attracted foreign capital inflows. Currency dealers reported heavy dollar selling by foreign banks and exporters. The Reserve Bank of India is expected to maintain its accommodative stance given the government's focus on growth. Bond markets also reacted positively with the 10-year government bond yield declining by 8 basis points to 6.92%. Foreign portfolio investors have turned bullish on Indian assets following the budget. Economists predict that sustained capital inflows could keep the rupee stable in the 82-83 range over the next quarter. However, global factors including US Federal Reserve policy and crude oil prices will continue to influence currency movements.

headline: Budget 2026: Cement, steel stocks rally on infrastructure push, analysts upgrade targets
body: Cement and steel stocks witnessed strong buying following the budget's massive infrastructure allocation. UltraTech Cement surged 7%, Ambuja Cement gained 6%, while Tata Steel and JSW Steel rose 5% each. The Rs 11.1 lakh crore capex allocation is expected to drive demand for construction materials. Analysts have upgraded earnings estimates for cement companies by 15-20% for FY27. Steel demand is projected to grow at 8-10% annually over the next three years. Brokerage firms have raised target prices for leading cement and steel companies. The government's focus on housing, highways, and metro projects will provide sustained demand visibility. However, experts cautioned that raw material costs and global commodity price fluctuations remain key risks. The sector is expected to see capacity expansion announcements in the coming quarters.

headline: Budget 2026 strikes right balance between growth and fiscal prudence: Chief Economic Advisor
body: Chief Economic Advisor Dr. V. Anantha Nageswaran praised Budget 2026 for striking the right balance between promoting growth and maintaining fiscal discipline. In an exclusive interview, he stated that the 4.5% fiscal deficit target is achievable given the strong tax revenue momentum and disinvestment pipeline. The focus on capital expenditure will have a multiplier effect on economic growth, potentially adding 0.5-0.7 percentage points to GDP. Dr. Nageswaran emphasized that infrastructure investment creates jobs, boosts demand, and improves productivity. He defended the tax reforms, stating they will increase disposable income and stimulate consumption. On inflation, he expressed confidence that it will remain within the RBI's target range of 4% (+/- 2%). The CEA also highlighted that India's growth story remains intact despite global headwinds, with domestic demand providing resilience.

headline: Budget disappoints on job creation front, lacks concrete employment schemes: Opposition economists
body: Opposition economists criticized Budget 2026 for lacking concrete measures to address India's unemployment crisis. Former Finance Minister P. Chidambaram stated that while infrastructure spending is important, it doesn't create enough jobs for India's young workforce. He pointed out that the budget has no major employment generation schemes beyond existing programs. Economist Jean Dreze argued that rural employment schemes need substantial enhancement given agrarian distress. Critics also highlighted that the manufacturing sector, which has the potential to create millions of jobs, received inadequate support. The absence of incentives for labor-intensive industries like textiles and leather was noted. Opposition parties demanded a separate employment guarantee scheme for urban youth. However, government economists countered that infrastructure investment indirectly creates substantial employment and that PLI schemes are already generating jobs in manufacturing.

headline: Budget 2026 positive for long-term growth, capex focus to boost economy: Moody's Analytics
body: Moody's Analytics released a positive assessment of India's Union Budget 2026, stating that the continued focus on capital expenditure will support long-term economic growth. The rating agency maintained India's growth forecast at 7.5% for 2026 and 7.2% for 2027. Moody's economist Rahul Bajoria noted that the fiscal consolidation path is credible and demonstrates the government's commitment to macroeconomic stability. The infrastructure push is expected to improve India's competitiveness and attract foreign investment. However, Moody's cautioned that execution risks remain, particularly in achieving the ambitious disinvestment targets. The agency also highlighted that private sector investment needs to pick up to sustain high growth rates. On the positive side, the tax reforms could boost consumption and support domestic demand. Moody's expects the Reserve Bank of India to maintain policy rates given the growth-inflation dynamics.

headline: Budget 2026: Goldman Sachs upgrades India to 'overweight', sees 15% upside in equities
body: Goldman Sachs upgraded India to 'overweight' in its Asia-Pacific portfolio following the Union Budget, citing strong growth prospects and reform momentum. The investment bank raised its Sensex target to 85,000 by December 2026, implying a 15% upside from current levels. Goldman's India strategist Sunil Koul stated that the budget's infrastructure focus, fiscal discipline, and tax reforms create a favorable environment for equities. The bank is overweight on financials, industrials, and capital goods sectors. Goldman expects corporate earnings to grow at 18% in FY27, driven by economic expansion and margin improvement. Foreign institutional investors are likely to increase India allocation given the positive policy environment. However, Goldman cautioned that global risk factors including US recession fears and geopolitical tensions could impact market sentiment. The bank maintains that India's domestic demand-driven growth model provides resilience against external shocks.

headline: Budget 2026 vs Budget 2020: How India's fiscal priorities have evolved post-pandemic
body: A comparison of Budget 2026 with Budget 2020 reveals a dramatic shift in India's fiscal priorities following the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2020, the fiscal deficit stood at 3.5% with total expenditure of Rs 30 lakh crore. Budget 2026 projects a fiscal deficit of 4.5% with expenditure of Rs 48 lakh crore, reflecting the massive expansion in government spending. Capital expenditure has more than doubled from Rs 4.1 lakh crore in 2020 to Rs 11.1 lakh crore in 2026. The focus has shifted from revenue expenditure to capital creation, with the capex-to-GDP ratio rising from 2.1% to 3.8%. Healthcare allocation has tripled from Rs 40,000 crore to Rs 1.2 lakh crore, reflecting lessons learned from the pandemic. Digital infrastructure, which received minimal attention in 2020, is now a priority with substantial allocations. Tax reforms have made the system simpler and more taxpayer-friendly. The disinvestment approach has become more strategic rather than opportunistic.

headline: India's capex journey: From Rs 4 lakh crore in 2020 to Rs 11 lakh crore in 2026
body: India's capital expenditure has witnessed unprecedented growth over the past six years, rising from Rs 4.1 lakh crore in Budget 2020 to Rs 11.1 lakh crore in Budget 2026. This represents a compound annual growth rate of 18%, far exceeding nominal GDP growth. The government's infrastructure-led growth strategy has transformed India's economic landscape. In 2020, highways construction averaged 25 km per day; by 2026, this has increased to 45 km per day. Railway electrification has jumped from 40% to 85% of the network. Metro rail networks have expanded from 700 km to 1,200 km across Indian cities. The number of airports has increased from 136 to 175. This massive infrastructure push has created millions of jobs and improved logistics efficiency. The government's focus on quality infrastructure is evident in the adoption of global standards and use of advanced construction technologies. Economists credit this capex boom for India's sustained high growth rates.

headline: Tax reforms timeline: How India simplified its tax structure from 2020 to 2026
body: India's tax system has undergone significant simplification between 2020 and 2026, making compliance easier and reducing litigation. In 2020, the new tax regime was introduced as an option alongside the old regime with deductions. Budget 2023 made the new regime the default option. Budget 2026 has further simplified tax slabs and increased the standard deduction. Corporate tax rates were reduced from 30% to 22% in 2019 and have remained stable since. GST compliance has been streamlined with quarterly returns for small businesses replacing monthly filings. The number of GST slabs has been reduced from six to four, eliminating confusion. Tax dispute resolution mechanisms have been strengthened with the Vivad se Vishwas scheme settling over 1 lakh cases. Faceless assessment and appeals have reduced corruption and improved efficiency. The tax-to-GDP ratio has improved from 10.8% in 2020 to 12.2% in 2026, demonstrating that simplification and lower rates can boost compliance and revenues.

headline: Budget 2026 sets stage for India to become $7 trillion economy by 2030: NITI Aayog
body: NITI Aayog Vice Chairman Suman Bery stated that Budget 2026 provides the policy framework for India to achieve a $7 trillion economy by 2030. The continued focus on infrastructure, manufacturing, and human capital development will drive sustained high growth. India's GDP is currently $3.8 trillion and needs to grow at an average of 8% annually to reach the $7 trillion target. The budget's emphasis on capital expenditure will crowd in private investment, which is crucial for achieving this goal. NITI Aayog projects that infrastructure investment will attract $500 billion in private capital over the next four years. The manufacturing sector is expected to contribute 20% of GDP by 2030, up from 17% currently, driven by PLI schemes and improved ease of doing business. Services exports, particularly in IT and business services, will continue to be a growth driver. However, Bery cautioned that global economic conditions and domestic reform implementation will be critical factors.

headline: What to watch after Budget 2026: RBI policy, monsoon, and global growth key factors
body: Following the Union Budget announcement, market participants will closely watch several key factors that will determine India's economic trajectory. The Reserve Bank of India's monetary policy meeting in April will be crucial, with expectations of a potential rate cut if inflation remains under control. The monsoon forecast, expected in May, will impact agricultural output and rural demand. Global economic conditions, particularly US growth and Federal Reserve policy, will influence foreign capital flows. Crude oil prices remain a wildcard, with any spike potentially affecting India's current account deficit and inflation. The government's execution of the disinvestment program will be monitored closely, as it's critical for meeting fiscal targets. Corporate earnings for Q4 FY26, to be announced in April-May, will provide insights into economic momentum. Infrastructure project implementation and private sector investment trends will indicate whether the budget's growth assumptions are realistic. Geopolitical developments, especially in the Middle East and Ukraine, could impact commodity prices and trade.

headline: Budget 2026 impact on common man: Higher take-home salary, but inflation concerns remain
body: Budget 2026 will put an additional Rs 40,000 annually in the hands of middle-class taxpayers through increased standard deduction and revised tax slabs. A person earning Rs 12 lakh annually will save approximately Rs 35,000 in taxes under the new regime. However, the absence of changes in GST rates means daily expenses remain unchanged. The increased allocation for healthcare and education will benefit families in the long run through better public services. Infrastructure development is expected to improve quality of life through better roads, metro connectivity, and reduced travel time. However, economists warn that if inflation rises above 5%, the real benefit of tax savings will be eroded. Food inflation, currently at 4.5%, remains a concern for lower-income households. The budget's focus on agriculture and rural development could improve farm incomes and boost rural demand. Overall, the budget is seen as middle-class friendly, but its success will depend on effective implementation and inflation management.

headline: Budget 2026: India's path to developed nation status by 2047 gets clearer roadmap
body: Budget 2026 provides a clearer roadmap for India's ambition to become a developed nation by 2047, the centenary of independence. The government has outlined specific targets: per capita income of $12,000 (from current $2,500), universal healthcare coverage, 100% literacy, and world-class infrastructure. To achieve these goals, India needs to sustain 8% annual growth for the next two decades. The budget's focus on education, healthcare, and infrastructure lays the foundation for this transformation. Manufacturing needs to grow at 12% annually to create jobs for India's young population. The government plans to invest $2 trillion in infrastructure over the next decade. Digital infrastructure will be critical, with plans for universal broadband coverage by 2030. Climate goals are integrated with development objectives, with 50% of energy from renewable sources by 2030. Experts believe that while the vision is ambitious, consistent policy implementation and political stability will be crucial for success.
